does: This paper systematically evaluates how large language models represent individuals from Global Majority nationalities in open-ended narrative generation tasks. The authors prompt widely-adopted LLMs to generate stories and analyze the resulting portrayals across multiple dimensions including stereotyping, erasure, and character agency. Their quantitative analysis reveals that minoritized national identities appear significantly underrepresented in neutral narratives while being dramatically overrepresented in subordinated roles—appearing over fifty times more frequently in such roles than in positions of power or authority. The work documents specific patterns of representational harm including one-dimensional characterizations and harmful stereotypes, demonstrating that these biases persist across multiple models and generation contexts.
connects: This research builds on emerging scholarship examining bias and fairness in language models, extending earlier work on demographic representation and stereotyping in neural text generation. It connects to broader literature on algorithmic bias and social harms in AI systems, while specifically addressing gaps in understanding how non-Western and Global Majority communities are portrayed by contemporary LLMs. The paper engages with critical perspectives on representational justice and harm from media studies and critical race theory, applying these frameworks to the novel domain of LLM-generated narrative. It also responds to growing concerns about high-stakes applications of LLMs in government and enterprise contexts where biased portrayals could have material consequences for vulnerable populations.
enables: This work establishes methodology and empirical grounding for auditing representational harms in generative models, enabling more rigorous evaluation of narrative bias across models and prompting strategies. The findings point toward interventions in model training, fine-tuning, and deployment—including data curation improvements, bias detection tools, and guardrails for high-stakes applications. By documenting specific patterns of harm by national origin, the research enables targeted mitigation efforts and supports policy discussions around responsible AI governance in global contexts. Future work can build on these frameworks to examine intersectional harms, develop better debiasing techniques, and ensure that systems used in consequential domains like asylum processing do not systematize existing inequities against Global Majority populations.
